From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Christian consecration of a building

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Invigning_Storvreta_Kyrka.jpg/250px-Invigning_Storvreta_Kyrka.jpg)](/wiki/File:Invigning_Storvreta_Kyrka.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Biskop_Karin_Johannesson_inviger_Storvreta_kyrka.jpg/250px-Biskop_Karin_Johannesson_inviger_Storvreta_kyrka.jpg)](/wiki/File:Biskop_Karin_Johannesson_inviger_Storvreta_kyrka.jpg)

The dedication of [Storvreta Evangelical-Lutheran Church](https://sv.wikipedia.org/wiki/Storvreta_kapell "sv:Storvreta kapell") by [Bishop Karin Johannesson](/wiki/Karin_Johannesson "Karin Johannesson"), the [Bishop of Uppsala](/wiki/Archdiocese_of_Uppsala "Archdiocese of Uppsala") of the [Church of Sweden](/wiki/Church_of_Sweden "Church of Sweden"), on [Pentecost Day](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost") 2024; in Evangelical-Lutheran practice, the [bishop](/wiki/Bishop "Bishop") knocks three times on the door of the church that is being dedicated with his/her [crozier](/wiki/Crozier "Crozier") while praying [Psalm 23](/wiki/Psalm_23 "Psalm 23") (left photograph). The bishop anoints the four walls of the church with [Holy Chrism](/wiki/Chrism "Chrism") in the dedication of churches (right photograph).

**Dedication of churches** is a Christian [dedication](/wiki/Dedication_(ritual) "Dedication (ritual)") ceremony of a [church](/wiki/Church_(building) "Church (building)") building. Churches under the authority of a [bishop](/wiki/Bishop "Bishop") are usually dedicated by that office holder.

## Consecration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dedication_of_churches&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Consecration")]

### Early customs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dedication_of_churches&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early customs")]

The custom of solemnly dedicating or consecrating buildings as [churches](/wiki/Church_(building) "Church (building)") or [chapels](/wiki/Chapel "Chapel") set apart for Christian worship must be almost as old as [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") itself. When we come to the earlier part of the 4th century allusions to and descriptions of the consecration of churches become plentiful.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWarren1911918-1)

This service is probably of Jewish origin: the hallowing of the tabernacle and of its furniture and ornaments (Exodus 40); the dedication of [Solomon's Temple](/wiki/Temple_of_Solomon "Temple of Solomon") (I Kings 8) and of the [Second Temple](/wiki/Second_Temple "Second Temple") by [Zerubbabel](/wiki/Zerubbabel "Zerubbabel") (Ezra 6); its rededication by [Judas Maccabaeus](/wiki/Judas_Maccabaeus "Judas Maccabaeus") (see above); the dedication of the temple of [Herod the Great](/wiki/Herod_the_Great "Herod the Great");[[2]](#cite_note-2) and Jesus' attendance at the Feast of Dedication ([John 10:22–23](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/John#10:22 "s:Bible (King James)/John")). All these point to the probability of the Christians deriving their custom from a Jewish origin.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWarren1911918-1)

[Eusebius of Caesarea](/wiki/Eusebius_of_Caesarea "Eusebius of Caesarea")[[3]](#cite_note-3) speaks of the dedication of churches rebuilt after the [Diocletian](/wiki/Diocletian "Diocletian") persecution, including the church at [Tyre](/wiki/Tyre_(Lebanon) "Tyre (Lebanon)") in 314 AD. The consecrations of the [Church of the Holy Sepulchre](/wiki/Church_of_the_Holy_Sepulchre "Church of the Holy Sepulchre") at Jerusalem in 335, which had been built by [Constantine I](/wiki/Constantine_I "Constantine I"), and of other churches after his time, are described both by Eusebius and by other [ecclesiastical historians](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_historians "Ecclesiastical historians"). From them we gather that every consecration was accompanied by a celebration of the [Holy Eucharist](/wiki/Holy_Eucharist "Holy Eucharist") and a [sermon](/wiki/Sermon "Sermon"), and special prayers of a dedicatory character, but there is no trace of the elaborate ritual of the medieval [pontificals](/wiki/Pontifical "Pontifical") dating from the 8th century onwards.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWarren1911918-1)

The separate consecration of altars is provided for by [Canon](/wiki/Canon_Law "Canon Law") 14 of the [Council of Agde](/wiki/Council_of_Agde "Council of Agde") in 506, and by Canon 26 of the [Council of Epaone](/wiki/Council_of_Epaone "Council of Epaone") in 517, the latter containing the first known reference to the usage of [anointing](/wiki/Anointing "Anointing") the altar with [chrism](/wiki/Chrism "Chrism"). The use of both [holy water](/wiki/Holy_water "Holy water") and of unction is attributed to [St. Columbanus](/wiki/Columbanus "Columbanus"), who died in 615.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWarren1911918-1)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

There was an annual commemoration of the original dedication of the church, a feast with its [octave](/wiki/Octave_(liturgical) "Octave (liturgical)") extending over eight days, during which [Gregory the Great](/wiki/Gregory_the_Great "Gregory the Great") encouraged the erection of booths and general feasting on the part of the populace, to compensate them for, and in some way to take the place of, abolished pagan festivities.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWarren1911918-1)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

At an early date the right to consecrate churches was reserved to [bishops](/wiki/Bishop "Bishop"), as by a canon of the [First Council of Bracara](/wiki/Councils_of_Braga "Councils of Braga") in 563, and by the 23rd of the Irish collections of canons, once attributed to [St Patrick](/wiki/St_Patrick "St Patrick"), but hardly to be put earlier than the 8th century.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWarren1911918-1)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

### Practice by Christian denomination

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dedication_of_churches&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Practice by Christian denomination")]

#### Eastern Orthodox form

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dedication_of_churches&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Eastern Orthodox form")]

Main article: [Consecration of an Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Consecration#Consecration_of_a_church "Consecration")

The dedication service of the [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") is likewise long and elaborate. At the beginning of construction, the [bishop](/wiki/Bishop "Bishop") or his deputy blesses a [cornerstone](/wiki/Cornerstone "Cornerstone") for the church. [Relics](/wiki/Relic "Relic") may be placed inside the cornerstone, and it will be topped with a plate giving the name of the [patron saint](/wiki/Patron_saint "Patron saint") of the new church, the names of the saints whose relics were deposited in the cornerstone (if any), the name of the ruling bishop, and the date.

After all construction on the building is finished, preparations are made for the solemn consecration of the church. The relics which will be placed in the [Holy Table](/wiki/Altar "Altar") (altar) and the [antimension](/wiki/Antimension "Antimension") are to be prepared and guarded on the previous day in some neighboring church (if there is no neighboring church, the relics are placed on a small table in front of the [icon](/wiki/Icon "Icon") of Christ on the [iconostasion](/wiki/Iconostasion "Iconostasion")). The night before the consecration, an [all-night vigil](/wiki/All-Night_Vigil "All-Night Vigil") is celebrated; however, no one will enter the [altar](/wiki/Sanctuary "Sanctuary") (sanctuary) of the new church yet, and the [Holy Doors](/wiki/Holy_Doors "Holy Doors") remain closed.

On the morning of the consecration, everything needed for the consecration, the sacred vessels, and all of the appurtenances of the sanctuary ([altar cloths](/wiki/Altar_cloth "Altar cloth"), candlesticks, etc.) are prepared on a table placed in front of the Holy Doors, together with a [Gospel Book](/wiki/Gospel_Book "Gospel Book") and blessing cross. The bishop (or his representative) and clergy [vest](/wiki/Vestments "Vestments") and proceed to the church. The clergy carry the table into the sanctuary and literally construct the Holy Table: the *mensa* (table top) is placed on the four pillars and four nails are driven in with stones. A prayer of dedication is said, followed by an *[ektenia](/wiki/Ektenia "Ektenia")* (litany). Warm water is poured thrice upon the Holy Table, and it is wiped down by the priests, and then washed with a mixture of [rose water](/wiki/Rose_water "Rose water") and red wine (signifying [baptism](/wiki/Baptism "Baptism")). It is then anointed with [chrism](/wiki/Chrism "Chrism") in the form of a cross (signifying [chrismation](/wiki/Chrismation "Chrismation")). The altar, the [Gospel Book](/wiki/Gospel_Book "Gospel Book"), and the [altar cloths](/wiki/Altar_cloth "Altar cloth") are then [censed](/wiki/Thurible "Thurible"), every pillar is crossed ([anointed](/wiki/Anointed "Anointed") in the [sign of the cross](/wiki/Sign_of_the_cross "Sign of the cross")) with chrism, while various hymns and psalms are chanted. The [sanctuary lamp](/wiki/Sanctuary_lamp "Sanctuary lamp") is then filled with oil and lit, and placed on or above the altar, while clergy bring in other lamps and other ornaments of the church.

Then, the bishop and clergy go to the neighboring church where the relics have been kept and guarded. A procession is formed and advances thence with the relics, which are borne by a priest in a [diskos](/wiki/Diskos "Diskos") (paten) on his head; the church having been entered, the relics are placed by him with much ceremonial in the [confession](/wiki/Confession_(religion) "Confession (religion)") (the recess prepared in or under the altar for their reception) which is then anointed and sealed up. After this the [Divine Liturgy](/wiki/Divine_Liturgy "Divine Liturgy") is celebrated both on the day of dedication and on seven days afterwards.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWarren1911919-7)

#### Catholic form

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dedication_of_churches&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Catholic form")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Pr%C3%BCfeninger_Weiheinschrift._Pic_01.jpg/250px-Pr%C3%BCfeninger_Weiheinschrift._Pic_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pr%C3%BCfeninger_Weiheinschrift._Pic_01.jpg)

[Latin dedicatory inscription of 1119](/wiki/Pr%C3%BCfening_dedicatory_inscription "Prüfening dedicatory inscription") for the church of [Prüfening Abbey](/wiki/Pr%C3%BCfening_Abbey "Prüfening Abbey"), [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Notre-Dame_de_la_Daurade_-_Alphabets.jpg/250px-Notre-Dame_de_la_Daurade_-_Alphabets.jpg)](/wiki/File:Notre-Dame_de_la_Daurade_-_Alphabets.jpg)

Mosaic showing the Greek and Latin alphabets in [Notre-Dame de la Daurade](/wiki/Notre-Dame_de_la_Daurade "Notre-Dame de la Daurade"), France

For the Catholic Church, the rite of dedication is described in the *[Caeremoniale Episcoporum](/wiki/Caeremoniale_Episcoporum "Caeremoniale Episcoporum")*, chapters IX-X, and in the *[Roman Missal](/wiki/Roman_Missal "Roman Missal")*'s Ritual Masses for the Dedication of a Church and an Altar.

The [manuscripts](/wiki/Manuscript "Manuscript") and printed service-books of the [medieval church](/wiki/Church_and_state_in_medieval_Europe "Church and state in medieval Europe") contain a lengthy and elaborate service for the consecration of churches in the pontifical. The earliest known pontifical is that of [Egbert, Archbishop of York](/wiki/Egbert,_Archbishop_of_York "Egbert, Archbishop of York") (732–766), which, however, only survives in a 10th-century manuscript copy. Later pontificals are numerous and somewhat varied. A good idea of the general character of the service can be obtained from a skeleton of it as performed in England after the [Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation") according to the [use of Sarum](/wiki/Sarum_Rite "Sarum Rite"). The service is taken from an early 15th-century pontifical in the [Cambridge University Library](/wiki/Cambridge_University_Library "Cambridge University Library") as printed by W. Makell in *Monumenta ritualia ecclesiae Anglicanae*.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWarren1911918-1)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

There is a preliminary office for laying a [foundation-stone](/wiki/Foundation-stone "Foundation-stone"). On the day of consecration the bishop is to vest in a tent outside the church, then proceed to the door of the church on the outside, a single deacon being inside the church. There he blesses holy water, twelve lighted candles being placed outside, and twelve inside the church. He then sprinkles the walls all round outside and knocks at the door. He then sprinkles the walls all round outside a second time, then a third time, knocking at the door each time. He may then enter, all laity being excluded. The bishop then fixes a cross in the centre of the church, after which the [litany](/wiki/Litany "Litany") is said, including a special clause for the consecration of the church and altar. Next the bishop inscribes the alphabet in Greek letters on one of the limbs of St Andrews cross from the left east corner to the right west corner on the pavement cindered for the purpose, and the alphabet in Latin on the other limb from the right east corner to the left west corner. He then genuflects before the altar or cross, blesses water, mingled with salt, ashes and wine, and sprinkles it on all the walls of the church inside thrice, beginning at the altar. He next sprinkles the centre of the church lengthwise and crosswise on the pavement and goes round the outside of the church sprinkling it three times. Next, reentering the church and taking up a central position, he sprinkles holy water to the four points of the compass, and up towards the roof. Next he anoints the twelve internal and twelve external [wall-crosses](/wiki/Consecration_crosses "Consecration crosses") with chrism before walking around the church three times inside and out and censing it.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWarren1911919-7)

Then there follows the consecration of the altar. First, [holy water](/wiki/Holy_water "Holy water") is blessed and mixed with [chrism](/wiki/Chrism "Chrism"). With the mixture the bishop makes a cross in the middle of the altar, then on the right and on the left, then on the four horns of the altar. The altar is then sprinkled seven times or three times with water not mixed with chrism and the altar table is washed, censed and wiped with a linen cloth. The centre of the altar is next anointed with the oil of the catechumens in the form of a cross. After the altar stone has been anointed with chrism, the whole altar is rubbed over with oil of the catechumens and with chrism. Incense is next blessed, and the altar censed, five grains of incense being placed crosswise in the centre and at the four corners. On the grains, five slender candle crosses are placed and lit. Afterwards the altar is scraped and cleansed. The altar cloths and ornaments are sprinkled with holy water and placed on the altar, which is then to be censed. All this is subsidiary to the celebration of Mass, with which the whole service is concluded. The transcription and description of the various collects, psalms, anthems and benedictions which make up the order of dedication have been omitted.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWarren1911919-7)

The Sarum order of dedication described above is substantially identical with the Roman order. There is, however, one very important and significant piece of ritual, not found in the English church order, but always found in the Roman service, and not infrequently found in the earlier and later English uses, in connection with the presence and use of [relics](/wiki/Relics "Relics") at the consecration of an altar. According to the Roman ritual, after the priest has sprinkled the walls of the church inside three times all round and then sprinkled the pavement from the altar to the porch, and sideways from wall to wall, and then to the four quarters of the compass, he prepares some cement at the altar. He then goes to the place where the relics are kept, and starts a solemn procession with the relics round the outside of the church. There a sermon is preached and two decrees of the [council of Trent](/wiki/Council_of_Trent "Council of Trent") are read together with the founder's deed of gift or endowment. Then the bishop, anointing the door with chrism, enters the church with the relics and deposits them in the cavity or confession in the altar. Having been enclosed they are censed and covered in, and the cover is anointed. Then follows the censing and wiping of the altar as in the Sarum order.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWarren1911919-7)

This use of relics is very ancient and can be traced back to the time of [St Ambrose](/wiki/St_Ambrose "St Ambrose"). There was also a custom, now obsolete, of enclosing a portion of the consecrated Eucharist if relics were not obtainable. This was ordered by cap. 2 of the [council of Celchyth](/wiki/Council_of_Celchyth "Council of Celchyth") (Chelsea) in 816. But though ancient, the custom of enclosing relics was not universal, and where found in English church orders, as it frequently is found from the pontifical of Egbert onwards, it is called the *Mos Romanus* as distinguished from the *Mos Anglicanus* (*Archaeologia*, liv. 416). It is absent from the description of the early Irish form of consecration preserved in the *[Leabhar Breac](/wiki/Leabhar_Breac "Leabhar Breac")*, translated and annotated by Rev. T. Olden.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWarren1911919-7)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The curious ritual act, technically known as the abecedarium, i.e. the tracing of the alphabet, sometimes in Latin characters, sometimes in Latin and Greek, sometimes, according to [Ménard](/wiki/Nicolas-Hugues_M%C3%A9nard "Nicolas-Hugues Ménard"), in Latin, Greek and Hebrew, along the limbs of St Andrews cross on the floor of the church, can be traced back to the 8th century and may be even older. Its origin and meaning are unknown. One explanation was suggested by Rossi and adopted by the [bishop of Salisbury](/wiki/Bishop_of_Salisbury "Bishop of Salisbury"). This interprets the St Andrew's cross as the initial Greek letter of Christus, and the whole act as significant of taking possession of the site to be consecrated in the name of Christ, who is the [Alpha and Omega](/wiki/Alpha_and_Omega "Alpha and Omega"), the word of God, combining in himself all letters that lie between them, every element of human speech. The three languages may then have been suggested by the Latin, Greek and Hebrew, in which his title was written on the cross.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWarren1911919-7)

The disentangling the Gallican from the Roman elements in the early Western forms of service was undertaken by [Louis Duchesne](/wiki/Louis_Duchesne "Louis Duchesne"), who shows how the former partook of a funerary and the latter of a baptismal character.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWarren1911919-7)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

##### Naming a church

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dedication_of_churches&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Naming a church")]

The way in which a Catholic church was officially given a name evolved in time.[[11]](#cite_note-Updike-11) In [England](/wiki/England "England") (with [Cornwall](/wiki/Cornwall "Cornwall")) and [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales"), the earliest churches were named after saints, and often after their founders, who, if eventually [canonised](/wiki/Canonization "Canonization"), seamlessly became the patron saint of the church.[[11]](#cite_note-Updike-11) Later on, churches were also dedicated to, and named for, the saints whose [relics](/wiki/Relic#Christianity "Relic") were displayed within.[[11]](#cite_note-Updike-11) Double dedications were based on the traditional association of two saints (i.e. SS Peter and Paul), or by the addition of a patron saint after the acquisition of relics, or by additional adoption of a universally recognised patron saint for a church initially dedicated to a local one.[[11]](#cite_note-Updike-11) The choice of the patron saint sometimes had to do with a connection between his or her attributes and those of the physical environment of the church, such as seafaring saints for churches located in ports, with the noted peculiarity that saints whose life details were the least known enjoyed the widest popularity in being chosen (i.e. [St George](/wiki/Saint_George#Historicity "Saint George") and [St Margaret](/wiki/Margaret_the_Virgin#Historicity "Margaret the Virgin")).[[11]](#cite_note-Updike-11)

#### Evangelical-Lutheran form

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dedication_of_churches&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Evangelical-Lutheran form")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Invigningsm%C3%A4ssa_Strovreta_Kyrka.jpg/250px-Invigningsm%C3%A4ssa_Strovreta_Kyrka.jpg)](/wiki/File:Invigningsm%C3%A4ssa_Strovreta_Kyrka.jpg)

The celebration of the Dedication [Mass](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy) "Mass (liturgy)") of [Storvreta Evangelical-Lutheran Church](https://sv.wikipedia.org/wiki/Storvreta_kapell "sv:Storvreta kapell") by [Bishop Karin Johannesson](/wiki/Karin_Johannesson "Karin Johannesson"), the [Bishop of Uppsala](/wiki/Archdiocese_of_Uppsala "Archdiocese of Uppsala") of the [Church of Sweden](/wiki/Church_of_Sweden "Church of Sweden"), on [Pentecost Day](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost") 2024.

In the [Evangelical-Lutheran Churches](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") of the historic episcopate, the dedication of a church includes the [bishop](/wiki/Bishop "Bishop") knocking on the door of the church three times, followed by the blessing of the [baptismal font](/wiki/Baptismal_font "Baptismal font"), [communion rail](/wiki/Communion_rail "Communion rail"), organ and pews.[[12]](#cite_note-Seifferlein2024-12)

#### Anglican forms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dedication_of_churches&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Anglican forms")]

Main article: [Dedications in the Church of England](/wiki/Dedications_in_the_Church_of_England "Dedications in the Church of England")

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section **is largely based on an article in the out-of-copyright [*Encyclopædia Britannica* Eleventh Edition](/wiki/Encyclop%C3%A6dia_Britannica_Eleventh_Edition "Encyclopædia Britannica Eleventh Edition"), which was produced in 1911.** It should be brought up to date to reflect subsequent history or scholarship (including the references, if any). When you have completed the review, replace this notice with a simple note on this article's talk page. *(April 2016)* |

There is no authorized form for the dedication of a church in the reformed [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"). A form was drawn up and approved by both houses of the [convocation](/wiki/Convocation "Convocation") of [Canterbury](/wiki/Canterbury "Canterbury") under [Archbishop Tenison](/wiki/Archbishop_Tenison "Archbishop Tenison") in 1712, and an almost identical form was submitted to convocation in 1715, but its consideration was not completed by the Lower House, and neither form ever received royal sanction.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWarren1911919-7)

The consequence has been that Anglican bishops have fallen back on their undefined *jus liturgicum*, and have drawn up and promulgated forms for use in their various [dioceses](/wiki/Diocese "Diocese"), some of them being content to borrow from other dioceses for this purpose. There is a general similarity, with a certain amount of difference in detail, in these various forms. In the [Diocese of London](/wiki/Diocese_of_London "Diocese of London") the bishop, attended by clergy and [churchwardens](/wiki/Churchwarden "Churchwarden"), receives outside the west door a [petition](/wiki/Petition "Petition") for consecration; the procession then moves round the whole church outside, while certain psalms are chanted. On again reaching the west door the bishop knocks thrice with his [crozier](/wiki/Crozier "Crozier"), and the door being opened the procession advances to the east end of the church, where prayers are said and the first [Eucharist](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist") celebrated.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWarren1911919–920-13)

#### Methodist forms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dedication_of_churches&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Methodist forms")]

The Methodist *[Book of Worship for Church and Home](/wiki/Book_of_Worship_for_Church_and_Home_(1965) "Book of Worship for Church and Home (1965)")* (1965) has the following Christian liturgies with respect to dedications: "An Office for the Dedication of a Church Building", "An Office for the Dedication of a School, College, or University Building", "An Office for the Dedication of a Hospital", "An Office for the Dedication of a Church Organ or Other Instruments for Sacred Music", and "An Office for the Dedication of a Memorial".[[14]](#cite_note-BOW1964-14)

In its ritual found in the *Discipline*, the [Allegheny Wesleyan Methodist Connection](/wiki/Allegheny_Wesleyan_Methodist_Connection "Allegheny Wesleyan Methodist Connection") includes a rite for the dedication of churches, as well as one for the dedication of parsonages.[[15]](#cite_note-AWMC2014-15)

The [Evangelical Wesleyan Church](/wiki/Evangelical_Wesleyan_Church "Evangelical Wesleyan Church"), in "The Ritual" part of its *Discipline*, contains a liturgy for the Dedication of Churches.[[16]](#cite_note-EWC2015-16)

#### Irvingian forms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dedication_of_churches&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Irvingian forms")]

In the [New Apostolic Church](/wiki/New_Apostolic_Church "New Apostolic Church"), the largest of the [Irvingian Churches](/wiki/Irvingian_Church "Irvingian Church"), a new church is dedicated during the celebration of the first Divine Service held therein.[[17]](#cite_note-NAC2020-17) *The Catechsim of the New Apostolic Church* teaches:

> The dedication service is based upon a Bible text that is in keeping with the occasion. The introductory words of the officiant express gratitude to God. In most cases, thanks is also expressed to the members for their willingness to make sacrifices, thus enabling the church to be built, as well as to all those who worked on its construction. The congregation's historical development is likewise addressed.
>
> In the dedication prayer, the house of God is dedicated to its sacred purpose in the name of the triune God. Thus the new church is consecrated as a place where the Holy Spirit reveals Himself. Here the word of God will henceforth be proclaimed, and here the sacraments will be dispensed. All activities performed in this house are to serve for the perfection of souls longing for salvation, and to prepare them for the return of Jesus Christ. The church building and all who gather there are commended to God's protection and to the service of His angels.
>
> The dedicated church is now a place for the worship of God and a sanctuary for those who seek salvation. It serves to offer them divine comfort, strength of faith, and peace of the soul in the divine services.[[17]](#cite_note-NAC2020-17)

#### Baptist and Pentecostal forms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dedication_of_churches&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Baptist and Pentecostal forms")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/KABA_2.jpg/330px-KABA_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:KABA_2.jpg)

Dedication [service](/wiki/Worship_service_(evangelicalism) "Worship service (evangelicalism)") at the [Kohima Ao Baptist Church](/wiki/Kohima_Ao_Baptist_Church "Kohima Ao Baptist Church") in [Kohima](/wiki/Kohima "Kohima"), affiliated to the [Nagaland Baptist Church Council](/wiki/Nagaland_Baptist_Church_Council "Nagaland Baptist Church Council"), 2019.

In evangelical [Baptist](/wiki/Baptist "Baptist") churches, the dedication of a church building usually takes place during a [service](/wiki/Church_service "Church service").[[18]](#cite_note-18) In [Pentecostal](/wiki/Pentecostal "Pentecostal") churches, a dedication plaque is also attached to the building.[[19]](#cite_note-19)
